title: Structured Output via tool_use & JSON Schemas

questions.question: You are building an extraction pipeline that processes legal contracts. Some contracts contain termination clauses and some do not. You need structured output that always includes a 'termination_clause' field. Which schema design prevents the model from fabricating clause text?

options.A: Mark termination_clause as required with type: "string"
options.B: Mark termination_clause as required with type: ["string", "null"] and a description stating to return null if absent
options.C: Remove termination_clause from the required array entirely
options.D: Add a system prompt instruction saying "do not invent termination clauses"

questions.explanation: Making the field required with a nullable type ensures the key is always present in the output (predictable structure) while giving the model permission to return null when the clause doesn't exist in the document. Option A forces fabrication. Option C means the key may be missing, complicating downstream parsing. Option D is unreliable against schema constraints.

questions.question: Your pipeline uses tool_choice: "auto" with an extract_metadata tool. In production, roughly 20% of documents receive a plain-text response instead of structured extraction. What change ensures structured output for every document?

options.A: Add "You must always use the extract_metadata tool" to the system prompt
options.C: Switch tool_choice to "any" and remove all other tools
options.D: Both B and C would work; B is more explicit

questions.explanation: Both B (forced tool selection) and C (any with a single tool) guarantee the model calls extract_metadata. B is preferred because it explicitly names the tool, making the intent clear and robust against future tool additions. A is unreliable because tool_choice: "auto" lets the model override prompt instructions.

questions.question: You define a tool with an enum field: "priority": {"enum": ["low", "medium", "high"]}. During testing, the model occasionally assigns "high" priority to items that are clearly low-priority based on the source text. What type of error is this?

options.A: A syntax error that JSON Schema should have caught
options.B: A semantic error — the value is schema-valid but factually wrong
options.C: A structural error — the field is in the wrong location
options.D: A format error — the enum type is misconfigured

questions.explanation: The value "high" is a valid enum option, so the schema is satisfied. However, it doesn't match the source document's intent. This is a semantic error — the output is structurally correct but factually incorrect. JSON Schema validation cannot detect this; you need a validation-retry loop or human review.

questions.question: You have three extraction tools: extract_invoice, extract_receipt, and extract_contract. Documents arrive with unknown types. Which tool_choice setting lets Claude pick the appropriate extraction schema while guaranteeing structured output?
questions.explanation: "any" forces the model to call one of the available tools but lets it choose which one based on the document content. "auto" doesn't guarantee a tool call. Forced selection (C) always calls extract_invoice regardless of document type. "none" disables tool use entirely.

questions.question: An extraction schema includes: "shipping_cost": {"type": "number"} as a required, non-nullable field. When processing a digital-download invoice that has no shipping, what is the most likely model behavior?

options.A: The API returns an error because the model cannot fill the field
options.B: The model returns shipping_cost: 0, which may be technically incorrect
options.C: The model omits the shipping_cost field from its response

questions.explanation: When a non-nullable number field is required, the model must provide a numeric value. It will likely output 0 — a plausible default, but semantically different from 'not applicable.' The value 0 means 'shipping was free,' not 'there was no shipping.' To distinguish these cases, make the field nullable so the model can return null when shipping doesn't apply.
